rg_summary: In Powell v. Emp. Dep't, 2026 Ore. App. LEXIS 171 (Ore. App. Feb. 11, 2026), the pro se claimant’s brief contained nonexistent legal citations, quotations that the court could not find “anywhere in Oregon case law,” and cases cited for propositions they did not support. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the claimant admitted that the errors were inadvertent and the result of GenAI. The claimant was previously admonished by a different court for inaccurate citations and had acknowledged that the tools he used led to incorrect citations. The court noted that even though the claimant was on notice that his drafting process could result in fabricated authority, he failed to alert this court about the potential issue or amend his briefs. Judge Joyce concluded that the claimant’s actions prejudiced the other side and wasted the court’s resources, imposing a $500 monetary sanction as a penalty. The court affirmed the Employment Appeals Board’s decision on unrelated grounds.

rg_summary: In Browne v. Ciobanu, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4128 (7th Cir. Feb. 10, 2026), in considering a motion for costs and fees, the court noted that the pro se plaintiff-appellant included nonexistent citations in her opposition brief. The panel reminded the plaintiff-appellant that pro se litigants who use GenAI to assist with court filings must make sure that their filings do not include misrepresentations of fact or law, and that further misrepresentations may result in sanctions.

rg_summary: In his Standing Order Regarding Use of Artificial Intelligence (90th Jud. Dist. Tex. Feb. 10, 2026), Judge Gregory addresses the use of AI in all pending and future cases in the 90th Judicial District Court for Young and Stephens Counties, Texas.  The order observes that GenAI systems “are being incorporated into common professional use,” but notes that the technology “is currently unreliable and prone to bias, and often fabricates information.” The order requires all self-represented litigants and attorneys who utilize “any form of artificial intelligence for legal research or drafting” to certify, using a form appended to the order, that all “language, quotations, sources, citations, arguments, and legal analysis created or contributed to by [GenAI]” were verified as accurate “through traditional (non-AI) legal sources” before submission.  Filers must also acknowledge that they will be “held responsible, and potentially sanctioned” for failure to comply with the order. The order also prohibits the use of AI in the courtroom and court offices, including the use of AI “to record, listen to, or transcribe any activity in the courtroom from whatever vantage point, or court office.”  The attached certificate for litigants makes clear that failure to comply may result in sanctions under the Texas Rules of Disciplinary Conduct, Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 10 regarding the withdrawal of an attorney, the inherent power of the court, or contempt of court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Labonte v. Bokf, N.A., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 27530 (D. Colo. Feb. 10, 2026), the pro se plaintiff's responses to defendants' motions to dismiss in a foreclosure-related action contained “multiple inaccuracies that may stem from the improper use of [GenAI].” The court provided several illustrative examples, including a nonexistent quotation attributed to a real case, a real case cited with an incorrect reporter that did not stand for the proposition asserted, and a real case misidentified as a Colorado court decision when it was in fact decided by another federal circuit and had no connection to the cited proposition. The court “strongly condemn[ed] the submission of inaccurate citations and mischaracterized authority,” finding that “[s]uch errors undermine the reliability of Plaintiff's filings and diminish Plaintiff’s credibility.” However, “[o]n careful consideration of the errors and circumstances of this case, including its dismissal,” the court declined to impose disciplinary sanctions. The court then granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In H.F. v. State of N.Y., 2026 NY Slip Op 26040 (N.Y. Ct. Cl. Feb. 10, 2026), the court “was unable to locate four Court of Claims cases cited” in the claimant’s Affirmation in Opposition to Defendant’s Motion, and other citations did not support the arguments made. Claimant’s counsel failed to respond to the court’s request for copies of the cases that it could not locate. The court struck the Affirmation from the record and warned that further submission of fictitious cases will result in monetary sanctions and a referral to the appropriate attorney grievance committee.

rg_summary: In Matter of Zareh, 2026 N.Y. App. Div. LEXIS 689 (N.Y. App. Div. Feb. 10, 2026), the court directed the respondent to demonstrate why reciprocal discipline should not be imposed in New York against the New York-barred respondent for GenAI misconduct which occurred and for which the respondent was disciplined in the Northern District of Texas. After an order to show cause, the district court had found that the drafting attorney, who the respondent supervised, filed a brief drafted using GenAI. The district court also found that although the respondent did not use GenAI or know that the drafting attorney used it, the respondent’s failure to review the cited cases “constituted bad faith” and was a violation of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The district court issued a public reprimand as sanction. In considering the Attorney Grievance Committee’s motion for reciprocal discipline, the New York court found that respondent had no available defenses: He received sufficient due process, there was adequate proof establishing misconduct, and the conduct for which the respondent was sanctioned constituted misconduct under the New York Rules of Professional Conduct. The court concluded that the appropriate sanction should be equivalent to that issued by the district court and ordered a public censure.

rg_summary: In McCarty v. Ind. Gaming Comm'n, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 54449 (S.D. Ind. Feb. 10, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response brief that included a citation the court could not locate. The court noted that it was “unclear why Plaintiff cited at least one case that does not appear to exist.” Although the court declined to impose sanctions, it cautioned all parties to adhere to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, “especially where [GenAI] may have been used to assist parties in preparing filings.” On unrelated grounds, the court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgement.

rg_summary: In Amarsingh v. Frontier Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 3928 (10th Cir. Feb. 9, 2026), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiff for her misuse of GenAI in researching and drafting her appellate brief. The plaintiff, an attorney representing herself, submitted a brief with serious flaws, including citations to nonexistent cases and attribution of propositions or quotations to real cases that did not stand for those propositions or contain the quotations. The court ordered the plaintiff to provide accurate copies of the suspect cases or explain how the fabricated citations ended up in her brief and to show cause why she should not be sanctioned. In response, the plaintiff admitted that she had used ChatGPT as a research and drafting aid, which generated the fabricated case citations, and acknowledged that she did not verify that the cases existed or that the quotations actually appeared in the cited cases. The court found that while there is “nothing inherently problematic with the use of GenAI in the practice of law, careless use of the tool can waste both judicial resources and the opposing party's time and money, and it can damage the credibility of the legal system.” Proceeding under Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 38, the court determined that the appeal was frivolous as argued, noting that an appeal may be frivolous as argued if supporting briefs contain “citations of inapplicable or irrelevant authorities” or “misrepresent[] facts or law to the court.” The court declined to impose the “harsh sanction” of dismissal but ordered the plaintiff to pay $1,000 to the defendant to offset attorney fees and costs incurred in responding to the problems identified in the plaintiff's brief. The court also directed the Clerk's Office to transmit a copy of its Order and Judgment to the attorney-disciplinary authority of the State of Maryland.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Bruce v. United States, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 26192 (D.S.C. Feb. 9, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s “filings . . . bear characteristics the court has increasingly observed in briefs generated by [GenAI] programs.” These characteristics included a nonexistent quotation from a local rule and other citation errors. Judge Lydon did not impose sanctions, but warned the plaintiff that he could be subject to sanctions should he cite to AI-generated legal authority in the future. The court adopted the Magistrate Judge’s R&R and granted the United States’ motion to dismiss without prejudice on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Tatum v. Am. Amicable Life Ins. Co. of Tex., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22283 (E.D. Tex. Jan. 9, 2026), a pro se plaintiff was accused of citing at least six nonexistent cases that appeared to be the product of GenAI. The court confirmed that it could not locate any of the cited cases, observing that the citations “make it impossible for these cases to exist because they would overlap with other existing cases.” The court emphasized that under Local Rule CV-11(g), litigants who use GenAI “remain[] responsible for the accuracy and quality of legal documents” and must verify all content for compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court further found that the plaintiff violated the court's standing order requiring certification of GenAI use. The court noted the harms of GenAI misuse, including wasted time and resources for opposing counsel and the court, potential reputational harm to judges and parties, and promotion of “cynicism about the legal profession and the American judicial system.” The court declined to consider the plaintiff's legal arguments supported by the fabricated citations and warned that any further GenAI misuse may result in sanctions under Rule 11.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Bettis v. Gaston, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 26147 (N.D. Ill. Feb. 9, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s response brief contained citations that the court identified as “hallmarks of [GenAI].” The court noted that the plaintiff cited a nonexistent case, a real case that did not support the asserted proposition, and a nonexistent quotation attributed to another real case. Citing recent Seventh Circuit authority, the court reminded the plaintiff that “all litigants—represented and unrepresented—must read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, factual and legal.” The court warned the plaintiff that future filings submitted without exercising reasonable care to check their accuracy may result in sanctions, finding that "[b]ased on the current available technology, relying on computer-generated language without verification is not reasonable care." The court granted the defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice on other grounds, finding the plaintiff's constitutional claims time-barred and his state law claim barred for lack of a private cause of action.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Khan v. NSO et al., No. 2:25-cv-11961 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 6, 2026), Judge Frimpong issued a resources document for the pro se plaintiff. The document notes that while the court does not restrict any party's use of AI, parties are reminded that AI “may output information that is false, inaccurate, or biased” and that it is the parties’ responsibility to “personally ensure the accuracy of any statements and representations made in the course of the litigation even if it has been generated by AI.” The court’s resources guide also directs the parties to the California State Bar's Practical Guidance on the use of AI.
rg_tags: Any AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI

rg_summary: In Berg v. United Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 25650 (D.  Colo. Feb. 6, 2026), the court denied the pro se plaintiff’s motion for extension and motion for reconsideration of Judge Prose’s prior order denying the plaintiff’s motion to compel discovery. Both Magistrate Judge Prose and Judge Wang had previously warned the plaintiff that her “filings contained erroneous and hallucinated citations consistent with misuse of [GenAI]” and that further violations could result in sanctions. Despite the warning, the plaintiff’s motion for extension cited to nonexistent and misrepresented cases. The court denied both motions on other grounds. Judge Wang imposed a filing restriction to counter the plaintiff’s “untimely, duplicative, and noncompliant” filings. The restriction prevented the plaintiff from filing any further motions without leave of court before the court rules on the defendant’s motion for summary judgment or vacates the order, noted that it will not grant leave without a certification that the use of AI is in compliance with the court’s standing order.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Davila v. Roblen, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 24971 (D. Conn. Feb. 6, 2026), the court addressed the conduct of the plaintiff's attorneys who filed a brief containing quotes generated by GenAI from legal authorities that did not contain those quotes. The court noted that one of the attorneys in question had previously been sanctioned by the court for submitting filings with GenAI hallucinations. See Cojom v. Roblen, LLC., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 225325 (D. Conn. Nov. 17, 2025). The plaintiff's lead attorney admitted that he used a GenAI tool, failed to verify the citations it produced, and based legal arguments on the fabricated quotes. The court found that this conduct constituted a “negligent[] . . . [failure] to perform [his] responsibility as an officer of the court” and negatively impacted the client, opposing parties, and the court itself by depriving the client of arguments based on legitimate precedent and requiring opposing counsel and the court to contend with “arguments based on phony law.” Noting the lead attorney’s acceptance of responsibility and commitment to remediation, the court ordered that he complete three hours of CLE on responsible AI use and publish a written article on the topic. The court declined to sanction co-counsel (who had requested to file a “corrected version” of the erroneous brief without disclosing that GenAI hallucinations were the reason for doing so), concluding that her lack of candor was not “a false statement of fact or law.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Mutugu v. Kiaraho, 2026 Ind. App. Unpub. LEXIS 203 (Ind. Ct. App. Feb. 6, 2026), the court dismissed the pro se appellant's appeal due to his “significant noncompliance with Indiana Appellate Rule 46,” which requires appellate briefs to be both accurate and follow certain filing guidelines, and awarded the appellee attorneys' fees. The appellant's brief was “deficient and violate[d] Appellate Rule 46(A) in almost every possible way,” including hallucinated and inaccurate citations. Of the 21 legal authorities cited in the appellant's argument, 5 cases did not exist, and 11 of the remaining authorities were “incorrectly cited or described.” Judge Felix observed that given the appellant's “citations to ‘nonexistent legal authorities and to real legal authorities that have nothing to do with the propositions they purport to support,’” it was likely that the appellant used GenAI to draft his brief. The court noted that “[c]itations to fictitious, AI-generated authority is a growing problem nationwide” and that “[c]ourts have sanctioned both attorneys and pro se litigants for including them in briefs.” Because the appellant's noncompliance substantially impeded appellate review and his appeal was “permeated with meritlessness,” the court granted the appellee's motion to dismiss, denied the appellant's motion to amend his brief, and remanded for a determination of reasonable appellate attorneys' fees.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Flycatcher Corp. v. Affable Ave. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23980 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 5, 2026), counsel for defendant submitted a brief containing at least thirteen hallucinated citations and eight faulty citations to caselaw that existed but did not stand for the proposition he claimed. When co-defendant's counsel alerted the attorney to these errors, he offered a convoluted excuse involving his use of CaseText, CoCounsel, and vLex for research and cite-checking, citing his lack of access to traditional legal research databases. After co-defendant’s counsel reported the attorney to the court, an order to show cause was issued. In response, the attorney filed a submission containing “conspicuously florid prose”, including extended quotes from irrelevant books of fiction, and a quote misattributed to a case when it came from an article discussing that case. Despite explicit warnings, the attorney then filed a proposed reply brief containing another nonexistent citation, which led to a sanctions hearing.At the sanctions hearing, the attorney was placed under oath and admitted to using various GenAI tools including vLex, Paxton AI, and NotebookLM, but “attempted to minimize his responsibility.” The court found that the attorney's AI misuse resulting in erroneous citations, exacerbated by his "insouciant approach to cite-checking," was done in bad faith, concluding that he knew or consciously avoided learning that the cases he cited were not warranted by existing law, and continuously presented to the court faulty, GenAI-created citations and briefs. The court weighed the five factors relevant to terminal sanctions, finding: (i) intentional bad faith; (ii) prejudice to the court and parties; (iii) a pattern of misbehavior rather than an isolated incident; (iv) failure to correct the misconduct; and (v) likelihood of future misconduct. Accordingly, pursuant to Rule 11, the court entered default judgment against the attorney's client, struck the defendant's filings, and granted leave for co-defendant's counsel to submit a fee application.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Odunze v. Lake Effect, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 24146 (D. Utah Feb. 4, 2026), the court addressed an opposition brief filed by the pro se plaintiff. The court noted that the plaintiff cited to a case that the court was “unable to locate.” Magistrate Judge Romero admonished the plaintiff that “[i]f artificial intelligence technology is being used,” he must “be in compliance with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11,” which requires that legal contentions be warranted by existing law. The court warned that “[c]ontinued misconduct of this nature may lead to sanctions.” On unrelated grounds, the court granted one of the defendant’s motions and denied the other as moot.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Rako v. VMware LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232131 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 25, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition briefs to the defendants’ motions to dismiss contained various “inadvertent citation errors,” which he did not attempt to correct until nearly six weeks after the defendants flagged these errors in their reply briefs. Judge Van Keulen reminded the plaintiff that all parties, regardless of their status of representation, must ensure that all assertions are verifiable and accurate. As such, the court ordered the plaintiff to include a footnote corresponding to “each citation located through AI” in all future filings that confirms that the authority is real and accurate (i.e., “Located through AI; Checked.”). On February 4, 2026, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23692, after observing the parties “weaponizing accusations regarding the use of AI” since her November order, the court imposed an additional requirement: all disputes over whether a party has properly used GenAI are now subject to the meet-and-confer and joint statement requirements that apply to discovery disputes under the court's standing order . Any disputes that cannot be resolved through good-faith conferral must be presented in a joint statement not to exceed five pages.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Kabir v. WebMD LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22400 (D.N.J. Feb. 3, 2026), the court addressed the pro se plaintiff's repeated non-compliance with filing rules and prior warnings about hallucinated citations. The plaintiff, who had filed numerous motions and voluminous submissions throughout the litigation, “repeatedly attempted to circumvent” applicable filing rules and directly contacted both the Chief Judge and the presiding judge in violation of the court's policies. The court revoked the plaintiff's electronic filing privileges and ordered the Clerk of Court not to docket any future submissions through the alternate document submission system. Judge Padin also noted that the plaintiff's opposition brief “relies on hallucinated quotations,” citing a nonexistent case, despite prior warnings that “[h]allucinated citations will not be excused” and that the court “may sanction Plaintiff for further uses of hallucinated citations.” The court reiterated its prior warning that hallucinated citations “will not be permitted under any circumstances.” Judge Padin cautioned that if the plaintiff continues to inappropriately rely on GenAI, he may be subject to a pre-filing injunction or other sanctions, and denied the plaintiff’s pending motion with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Lexos Media IP, LLC v. Overstock.com, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258649 (D. Kan. Dec. 15, 2025), the court granted in part and denied in part the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a corrected opposition to a Daubert motion after the defendant identified “nonexistent quotations,” “nonexistent or incorrect citations,” and “misrepresentations about cited authority” in the plaintiff’s original brief—errors the plaintiff attributed to counsel’s unverified use of GenAI. The court held that allowing substantive changes would function as an improper sur-reply and that Plaintiff failed to show excusable neglect under Fed. R. Civ. P. 6(b), emphasizing the prejudice to the defendant and the waste of judicial resources caused by the defective filing. Citing recent authority warning that careless use of GenAI can waste resources and undermine credibility, the court declined to permit a “do-over,” denied any additions of text or authority, and struck the original response. The court ordered Plaintiff to file a corrected brief by December 19, 2025 that omits all nonexistent quotations, nonexistent/incorrect/inapposite citations, and misrepresentations about cited authority. On February 2, 2026, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 20994, following the corrected brief’s filing, the court issued an order admonishing all five plaintiff attorneys and sanctioning four of them, imposing fines ranging from $1,000 to $5,000, revoking one attorney’s pro hac vice status and ordered him to self-report to the state’s legal disciplinary authority, ordering the managing member of the law firm to create a more effective GenAI firm policy, and accepting local counsel’s “voluntarily sanction[]” of not serving as local counsel for a year. The court declined to sanction the fifth attorney, the “most junior attorney on this matter,” as he had no responsibility to review his supervisors’ work.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Am. Express Nat'l Bank v. Flippin, 2026 Ga. State LEXIS 185 (Ga. Cnty. Ct. Feb. 2, 2026), the pro se defendant filed an “avalanche” of GenAI-generated motions, notices, responses, and other filings in a debt collection case, totaling approximately sixty or more distinct filings since October 2025. The court had previously entered a standing order requiring any party who uses AI to disclose such use and certify that every citation has been verified as accurate, applicable to all parties including pro se litigants. Despite including certifications of compliance with the Standing Order, the defendant’s filings were “replete with manufactured arguments and hallucinations,” invoking both real cases that did not support the propositions for which they were cited and “fictitious authority pulled from thin air.” The struck the defendant’s motion to compel from the record after concluding that it was “based exclusively on two nonexistent cases.” Although the defendant admitted to having submitted nine fictitious cases in good faith, the court observed that his “torrent of filings continued with frivolous arguments and misrepresentations of the law, which are just as troublesome.” Because of the defendant’s pro se status and his good faith correction concerning the fictitious cases, the court declined to impose sanctions but warned that “future abuses in this Court or others may not be met with such lenience.” On unrelated grounds, the court entered judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In her updated Standing Order for Civil and Discovery Referral Matters (N.D. Cal. Feb. 2, 2026), Magistrate Judge Susan van Keulen sets forth procedures applicable to civil and discovery matters before her court in the Northern District of California, including provisions governing GenAI use. The standing order provides that the signature of counsel or a self-represented party on any submission containing GenAI-generated content, including citations generated by GenAI, constitutes a certification that the signing attorney or self-represented party has personally verified the content's accuracy. The court will impute any errors by GenAI tools to the attorney or party whose signature appears on the document containing those errors. The order warns that the failure to verify the accuracy of submissions, particularly the accuracy of citations to law and evidence, may be grounds for sanctions. Further, the order requires counsel to maintain records of all prompts or inquiries submitted to any GenAI tools in the event those records become relevant “at any point.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Smith v. Kreeger, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22187 (S.D. Fla. Feb. 2, 2026), a pro se plaintiff filed a complaint challenging state court custody proceedings. The court observed that the plaintiff's complaint relied on “non-existent case citations that appear to have been generated by [GenAI].” The court warned that the plaintiff's “pro se status does not absolve [her] of the responsibility to abide by a duty of candor to the Court” and emphasized that sanctions against parties who submit fabricated citations, including dismissal, are not uncommon. On unrelated grounds, the court recommended the denial of the plaintiff’s motion.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Schaaf v. Nellis Auction Holdings, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22121 (D. Nev. Jan. 30, 2026), the court addressed plaintiff counsel’s continued misconduct, including hallucinated citations in three briefs stemming from the use of GenAI. In declining to impose terminating sanctions, the court acknowledged that case-dispositive sanctions are reserved for extreme situations and distinguished this case from one in which the complaint itself contained potentially hallucinated factual allegations. The court explained that here, the defendants pointed only to incorrect citations in briefing that had already been addressed through the underlying motion practice. The court, however, admonished plaintiff's counsel for his “lapse in his duties as an officer of the Court,” warning that continued misuse of GenAI would result in serious sanctions, including “case-dispositive sanctions imposed on his clients,” monetary penalties, and referral to the state bar. The court concluded by denying the defendants’ motion for case-terminating sanctions, motion to strike, and motion to stay.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Mills v. City of St. Louis, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 19989 (E.D. Mo. Jan. 30, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff used GenAI in preparing filings, which “contained misquotations or misrepresentations about case law he cited and discussed” with the “egregiousness of these misstatements increas[ing] over time.” In response to the order to show cause, the plaintiff admitted to using “electronic drafting tools that paraphrase legal principles.” Judge Schelp concluded that the plaintiff violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 by submitting fictitious and misleading cases to the court, regardless of whether the plaintiff used GenAI. In determining the appropriate sanction, the court considered that the plaintiff “made numerous misstatements in multiple filings,” continued making false representations after the court put the plaintiff on notice, and is well-educated and thus “should have known better.” Judge Schelp therefore reasoned that an “admonition, reprimand, or censure” would not sufficiently deter plaintiff from repeating this conduct, so the court dismissed the action with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In SEC v. Nantomah, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 18126 (E.D. Wis. Jan. 30, 2026), the court noted that the pro se defendant’s citations in his response to the SEC’s complaint “bear the hallmarks of generative artificial intelligence.” Judge Pepper reminded the defendant of his obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and warned that future filings that “contain citations to nonexistent cases or that quote language that does not appear in a cited case—whether the document is AI-generated or not” may result in sanctions. The court granted the SEC’s motion to strike the defendant’s answer on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Wilcox v. Gingrinch, 2026 Ind. App. LEXIS 29 (Ind. Ct. App. Jan. 30, 2026), the pro se appellants cited a “substantial number” of cases that “either do not exist or have nothing to do with the propositions they purport to support.” The court observed that the “pattern and nature of the citation errors in the [] brief strongly suggest the use of [GenAI] without adequate verification.” Furthermore, Judge May could not evaluate the merits of the appellants’ primary legal argument because it was based on fabricated authorities. The court therefore considered those issues waived, noting that “[h]ad the [plaintiffs] performed basic verification, the waiver we find today could have been avoided.” Because this was the appellants’ first infraction, Judge May did not impose sanctions, but cautioned that “the submission of briefs citing nonexistent authorities in future proceedings may result in sanctions.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Greene v. GSK PLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 17248 (W.D. Wash. Jan. 29, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited “several authorities which do not support her proposition or do not contain the text which she quotes” in her motion for court-assisted service, leading the court to suspect GenAI misuse. Judge Cartwright reminded the plaintiff that she is bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 which requires that “legal contentions are warranted by existing law…” and warned that she could face sanctions if she submits nonexistent authority to the court in the future. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Mims v. Brown, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23032 (W.D. Wis. Jan. 29, 2026), the court addressed filings by the pro se plaintiff that contained indications of GenAI use. The court noted that several of the plaintiff's recent filings had metadata in PDF files listing ChatGPT as the source and one document bearing the ChatGPT logo. Judge Peterson warned the plaintiff to “proceed with caution,” observing that GenAI-generated court filings “often contain inaccurate factual representations, 'hallucinated' citations to non-existent cases, or fake quotations.” The court emphasized that all litigants, regardless of representation status, have an obligation to “read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, whether factual or legal,” and that failure to do so may result in sanctions under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, “including dismissal of their case with prejudice.” Judge Peterson encouraged the plaintiff not to use GenAI to draft submissions to the court, noting that even when such documents do not contain misrepresentations, “AI-generated legal documents often appear formal and legalistic, but contain very little helpful substance.” The court advised that it would be more helpful for the plaintiff to explain his position “using simple, everyday language, even if the result is that his filing looks less like a formal legal document drafted by a lawyer.” Judge Peterson concluded by reminding the plaintiff that if he does choose to use GenAI, he must still review his documents before filing and ensure that the facts and the law are correct, as he remains responsible for the content of documents filed in court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Berg v. United Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 15973 (D. Colo. Jan. 28, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff used GenAI in her Motion to Compel Discovery because it included citations to “seemingly nonexistent cases.” The plaintiff included such citations despite the fact that she had also “included an artificial intelligence certification with each of her motions,” and the court had warned her on “multiple occasions to either avoid using artificial intelligence or to very carefully review her filings in order to avoid this exact scenario.”  Magistrate Judge Prose did not impose sanctions but warned that she will consider recommending dismissal or other sanctions if the plaintiff repeats this behavior in the future
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In his Standing Order on Use of Artificial Intelligence (“AI”) in Preparing Court Filings (D. Kan. Jan. 28, 2026), Chief Judge Broomes reminds litigants that “AI tools can produce false statements of fact or law, including citations to fabricated or incorrect legal authority.” The court warns that “all lawyers and parties, including pro se litigants . . . . are responsible for the content of their own filings,” must verify all content “drafted or assisted by an AI tool,” and may not make or fail to correct “a false or misleading statement of material fact or law.” The court has discretion to require a party to include a sworn statements disclosing the extent of AI use and certifying personal verification of the filing’s contents. Violation of the standing order could result in sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Walder v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34005 (E.D. Tex. Jan. 27, 2026), Magistrate Judge K. Nicole Mitchell recommended denying the pro se plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment in a Fair Credit Reporting Act case and warned the plaintiff about her citation to fictitious cases. The defendant pointed out in its joint response that many of the cases cited in the plaintiff’s motions either did not exist or did not support the plaintiff’s propositions, suggesting the citations may have been the result of GenAI “hallucinations.” The court confirmed that it was unable to locate any of the three cases the plaintiff cited in her motion. Magistrate Judge Mitchell reminded the plaintiff that Local Rule CV-11(g) warns litigants that GenAI “may produce factually or legally inaccurate content” and requires litigants to “review and verify all content” for compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court emphasized that filing papers with citations to nonexistent law is “unacceptable” and that a “fake opinion is not ‘existing law.’” In light of the plaintiff's pro se status and consistent with other courts' treatment of similar issues, the court declined to impose sanctions but warned the plaintiff that further citation to nonexistent cases and noncompliance with Local Rule CV-11(g) may result in sanctions, “including her pleadings and other papers being struck, filing restrictions, dismissal of her claims, and monetary penalties.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Cassata v Michael Macrina Architect, P.C., 2026 N.Y. Misc. LEXIS 325 (N.Y. Sup. C. Jan. 27, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel alleged that the defendant’s counsel plagiarized a brief that itself was improperly drafted using GenAI. In response to the order to show cause why the defendant’s attorney, supervising attorney, and law firm should not be sanctioned, the law firm stated that the attorney no longer worked at the firm and the supervising attorney had “no indication” that the attorney’s “work was plagiarized or improperly relied upon by [GenAI].” He further stated that the firm conducted additional training on the importance of compliance with the GenAI provisions in the firm handbook. At the hearing, the defendant’s attorney denied GenAI use in research but “appeared sincerely sorry” and admitted that she “found a brief on Westlaw which she incorporated into her opposition without verifying the citations, quotations, and propositions of law.” The court found this unpersuasive, and concluded that “citing non-existent cases, quotations, and specific propositions of law for which they do not stand and/or do not support, is unethical and sanctionable conduct” regardless of GenAI use. The courts considered the defense attorneys’ apologies, request for a “redo,” and inexperience as an attorney, ultimately concluding that sanctions were still warranted. In determining the amount of sanctions, the court found that the defendant’s attorney failed to verify cited authority before filing her opposition; that the attorney, supervising attorney, and the law firm “failed to promptly correct the record once inaccuracies were discovered”; and that the firm and supervising attorney lacked proper oversight and safeguards to prevent further misconduct. The court fined both the attorney and supervising attorney $1,000 (payable to the Lawyer’s Fund for Client Protection) and awarded $8,000 in attorney’s fees payable by the defendant’s law firm. The court further ordered that the attorney serve a copy of the decision and order on the clerk of court and Lawyer’s Fund for Client Protection.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: Chief Judge Phillips’ standing order titled, “Use of Artificial Intelligence in Court Filings” permits the use of GenAI to assist in preparation, research, drafting of pleadings and documents, filing, and discovery, but requires that all GenAI-generated information have “appropriate human oversight,” including “checking citations, verifying factual claims, and analyzing conclusions.” The order further requires that any filing prepared with GenAI assistance identify the specific tool used and include a certification attesting to their personal review and independent verification of the filing’s accuracy, noting that failure to do so could result in sanctions (as can the submission of “false or misleading AI-generated content”). The order also emphasizes that attorneys may use GenAI only to the extent they can reasonably guarantee compliance with their ethical obligations under the Rules Regulating the Florida Bar. The order reminds pro se litigants that they must be aware of GenAI's propensity to generate fictitious legal authorities and must ensure such citations do not appear in court filings.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Lifetime Well LLC v. IBSpot.com Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 13363 (E.D. Pa. Jan. 26, 2026), the court identified a briefing “contain[ing] at least eight false case citation hallucinations generated by artificial intelligence” submitted jointly by out-of-state counsel and local co-counsel for plaintiffs. In response, the court issued a show cause order, after which both sets of counsel admitted lapses in diligence tied to GenAI use. The out-of-state counsel terminated at least three attorneys responsible for preparing the briefing in response to the show cause order on her own accord, an action the court criticized by stating, “[the senior lawyer] did not explain why she did not use this mistake as a lesson rather than a reason to fire the new lawyer.” During the senior attorney’s investigation of her subordinate’s behavior, she found that the junior attorney relied on Lexis+ AI and LexisNexis Protégé as their primary research tools. The court also noted the peril out-of-state counsel placed local counsel in by asking him to sign and file the motion without diligently checking its citations, while admonishing local counsel for failing to properly vet the briefing before submitting it to the court. After learning of the filing’s deficiencies, local counsel voluntarily took CLE courses related to GenAI, reviewed all recent decisions from the Third Circuit and other courts related to GenAI misuse, and made a new agreement with out-of-state counsel to allow for ample review time of all motions prior to future filings with the court. The court found that both attorneys violated Rule 11 by submitting false citations and quotations without independently verifying the authorities, emphasizing that the signing attorney remains the final auditor of all legal and factual assertions. As tailored deterrence, the court imposed a $4,000 fine on the out-of-state attorney personally and required remedial educational outreach (including providing a copy of the court’s memorandum and order, the out-of-state counsel’s new GenAI firm-wide policy, and a cover letter to both local legal organizations and justices from other courts to whom the subordinate attorney previously submitted hallucinated citations to). The court imposed non-monetary sanctions on local counsel in the form of similar outreach to a local bar association. The court declined to refer either attorney to their respective bar disciplinary panels, and reminded all attorneys to use caution when submitting authority to the court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Future Field Sols., LLC v. Norstrand, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 12278 (D. Md. Jan. 23, 2026), the court addressed defendants’ request for a show cause order and sanctions premised on indications that plaintiff’s reply brief was drafted using GenAI (including “incorrect case citations, unusual grammatical errors, responses to arguments [not made], and false factual assertions”). In ruling on the motion, the court explained that no rule forbids GenAI, but emphasized that Rule 11(b) requires a reasonable pre filing inquiry and that submissions drafted with GenAI without appropriate verification are sanctionable, further noting that courts are more likely to impose sanctions “when a party doubles down” on denials “rather than accept responsibility for doing so at the first opportunity.” The court found plaintiff counsel’s response to the defendants’ request did not fully address the accusations and, by partially shifting blame to the client, suggested insufficient attorney oversight. Although the court declined to issue a show cause order and denied the sanctions request without prejudice, Judge Chasanow noted that the issue of sanctions may be revisited later if appropriate. On unrelated grounds, the court granted summary judgment to the third party defendant and largely to the counterclaim defendants, denied the counterclaim plaintiff’s cross motions, dismissed any remaining claims, and ordered the company to show cause why default should not be entered.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Olali v. Ampex Brands, LLC, No. 3:25-CV-1602-K-BK, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 37552 (N.D. Tex. Jan. 23, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff prepared his filings using GenAI because he cited to nonexistent cases, but failed to disclose that GenAI use, in violation of local rules. The court noted that this conduct also violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court found that the plaintiff’s “filings—built on AI-generated cases that stand for legal propositions in direct contravention of actual case law—are ‘the epitome of baseless.’” Magistrate Judge Toliver recommended granting the defendant’s motion to dismiss with prejudice, in part based on the GenAI misuse, and warned that further violations may result in sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Tolbert v. State, 2026 Ga. App. LEXIS 21 (Ga. Ct. App. Jan. 22, 2026), the court found the appellant’s brief rife with briefing defects, including reliance on irrelevant or overruled authority, quotations that do not appear in the cited cases, and material misrepresentations of the record. Although the court declined to make “factual findings here as to how or why [plaintiff] provided inappropriate authority to this Court,” notably not accusing him of misusing GenAI, the court concluded that “such misrepresentations exist and this appeal is frivolous as a result.” The court then ordered the appellant’s counsel to serve the opinion on his client and the State Bar of Georgia, file a certificate of compliance within ten days, and complete three hours of CLE on brief writing before filing any further briefs, warning that future violations may trigger contempt and jeopardize his authority to practice, and noting that monetary sanctions are not authorized in criminal cases. The court affirmed the judgment, in part because of the brief’s deficiencies.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Judge Kenneth B. Hodges | Judge Presiding J. McFadden | Judge John Andrew Pipkin

rg_summary: In Campbell v. Campbell, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 475 (Tex. App. Jan. 22, 2026), the pro se appellant filed motions that cited authorities that do not exist and purported quotations that do not appear in the referenced opinions—conduct the court observed suggests use of GenAI. The court cautioned that pro se and represented litigants are held to the same rules and that use of hallucinated citations can lead to serious consequences, citing decisions that have imposed significant sanctions for such misuse. The court dismissed the appeal on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Gisela D. Triana | Judge Chari L. Kelly | Judge Maggie Ellis
court: Tex. Ct. App., Third Dist., Austin

rg_summary: In That Xiong v. Wofford, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 12054 (E.D. Cal. Jan. 21, 2026), the court flagged two citations in a briefing filed by petitioner’s counsel (which included a certified law student and a supervising attorney) that “appeared to be [GenAI] hallucinations” and directed counsel—via a minute order—to be prepared to address them at a hearing. Petitioner’s counsel promptly filed a notice admitting error, apologizing, and supplying citations to actual, relevant cases in place of the hallucinated authorities. At the hearing, the court addressed the hallucinated citations and imposed corrective measures, including ordering counsel to obtain the transcript, provide it to the law student and any staff who assisted with the filings, require them to read it, and file written confirmation. On unrelated grounds, the court denied the motion.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Parker v. Labat, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 11877 (N.D. Ga. Jan. 21, 2026), the court issued a show‑cause order after the plaintiff’s brief contained “misstatements of law, non‑existent quotations, and non-existent case citations”—conduct the court emphasized is sanctionable whether stemming from GenAI or “old‑fashioned” attorney error, because citations submitted to the court must be warranted by existing law. The order required a sworn explanation and a statement disclosing whether counsel used GenAI, yet counsel failed to respond in any way. Concluding that the submission of fabricated or misrepresented authorities and the assertion of frivolous claims violated Rule 11(b), the court recommended sanctions, including a monetary fine of $1,500, mandatory CLE, and a sworn verification in future filings attesting that all citations and quotations were personally checked for accuracy. On unrelated grounds, the court recommended dismissing the plaintiff’s complaint.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Jian Lu v. USCIS, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9666 (D. Nev. Jan. 20, 2026), the pro se petitioners included both AI prompts given to a GenAI tool and the GenAI tool’s incorrect legal arguments in their motion for reconsideration. The court recognized that AI is a powerful tool that can assist pro se litigants but flagged that the petitioners here “treaded into perilous territory” based on their “clear ‘cut and paste’ arguments” and lack of supporting legal authority. Though the motion was ultimately denied on the merits, Judge Silva warned the petitioners that they must comply with “applicable law and procedural rules,” including Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b)(2)’s requirement that all claims “are warranted by existing law.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Med. Buyers Grp., LLC v. Pence, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9814 (M.D. Ga. Jan. 20, 2026), the defendants identified hallucinated cases cited by the plaintiff in its response to the defendants’ motion to dismiss. In response to an initial order to show cause as to why sanctions should not be granted, plaintiff’s counsel admitted to submitting hallucinated cases and explained what he had done to remediate this error, including (i) informing his client of the error, (ii) asking defendants’ counsel to provide attorney’s fees for the matter; (iii) confirming the accuracy of the other citations in its briefing; (iv) drafting amended responses; (v) modifying the firm’s cite check procedures; and (vi) attending a CLE course on “Common AI Mistakes.” As the defendants did not separately move for sanctions as required by Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(c)(2), the court imposed sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel based on its inherent authority. Judge Self acknowledged plaintiff’s counsel “sincer[e] . . . apology” but indicated that even unintentionally citing hallucinated cases constitutes “bad faith.” As such, the court ordered plaintiff’s counsel to pay $10,000 in attorney’s fees divided in half between the defendants’ two sets of attorneys.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Lattimore v. Tri-State Auto Sales, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 10013 (S.D. Ohio Jan. 20, 2026), the court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss the pro se plaintiff’s complaint and flagged in a footnote that the plaintiff admitted to using an unidentified GenAI tool “in preparation of his objections” to the magistrate judge’s report and recommendations. Notwithstanding his pro se status, Judge Hopkins reminded the plaintiff of his obligations to adhere to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b) and warned that he could face sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Button v. Jimison, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 205291 (W.D. Wash. Oct. 17, 2025), the defendant, in his response to pro se plaintiffs’ motion to strike his answer, identified that plaintiffs’ motion included hallucinated quotes and cases. In response the court warned plaintiffs that “they are responsible for verifying that all information and all citations submitted are accurate and real pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11,” recommended that plaintiff “should not rely on [AI] programs to conduct research or draft legal arguments, and ordered that all of plaintiffs’ future filings include a signed certification that states “I have reviewed each court decision cited in this filing. I certify that each decision cited is a valid decision and that any language quoted from a decision that is included in this filing is accurate.” The court further warned that if any of plaintiffs’ future pleadings do not include the certification, they will be stricken.Since this warning, the plaintiffs filed four subsequent motions and pleadings, all of which included the required certification. However, two of the pleadings nonetheless contained hallucinated and inaccurate citations. As a result, on January 20, 2026, the court struck the two pleadings that contained inaccurate citations in accordance with the court’s prior warning, noted that two other courts have recently admonished the plaintiffs for similar conduct, and warned that further filing of fictious citations may lead to the imposition of sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Tafah v. Lake Vill. Townhomes, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 8657 (D. Md. Jan. 16, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiffs cited to “inaccurate or non-existent” cases in the amended complaint. Judge Hurson cautioned that the plaintiffs “may face consequences if they continue to cite to non-existent cases in future litigation.” The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Tiffany K. v. Comm'r of Soc. Sec., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9392 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 16, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel cited to a nonexistent case in her briefing “likely caused by use of [GenAI].” Judge Patti warned counsel and her law firm that “citations of this sort and unconfirmed use of AI have led to sanctions” and that “future instances of this will not be tolerated.” Judge Patti granted the plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment and remanded the case to the Commissioner of Social Security on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Lepp v. Mallett, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9446 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 16, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an exhibit in support of her Motion for Relief from Judgment that contained an “objective and dispassionate review of the Court's [R&R]” performed by ChatGPT. The court declined to consider the Report in part because legal arguments are not appropriately filed in an exhibit. Furthermore, the court found the AI-generated legal report inaccurate and unreliable and that it contained an incorrect discussion of caselaw. Judge Levy warned that submissions with inaccurate citations could result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for relief from judgement on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Riverchase, LLC v. Goldwyn, 2026 Kan. App. Unpub. LEXIS 17 (Kan. App. Jan. 16, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s brief “contains numerous citation errors, mischaracterizations, and even citations to cases that appear not to exist or have no relevance” to the matter at issue. Judge Arnold-Burger reminded the parties that pro se litigants must follow procedural rules and held that regardless of whether the plaintiff used GenAI, the plaintiff has “waived and abandoned” her arguments “due to wholly inadequate briefing.” The court warned that while it did not impose consequences, the briefing errors justified dismissing the claims and imposing sanctions. The court affirmed the default judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Chief Justice Sarah E. Warner | Judge Karen Arnold-Burger | Judge Rachel L. Pickering

rg_summary: In Hobbs v. Goncharko, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9005 (N.D. Ill. Jan. 16, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited to a nonexistent case in his response brief. The plaintiff had cited to the same nonexistent case in a prior filing and the court had warned that sanctions could result. The court declined to impose sanctions for this repeated infraction but warned the plaintiff that another citation to a nonexistent case could result in dismissal of his case with prejudice. Judge Jenkins denied the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: Judge Orshan’s standing order titled, “Disclosure of use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Attorneys and Self-Represented Litigants” requires attorneys and self-represented litigants to disclose the use of GenAI in the “preparation” of various court filings, include a certification stating that GenAI was used, attest that all factual assertions, legal authority, and citations have been independently reviewed and verified for accuracy, and acknowledge that the filer accepts full responsibility for the contents of the filing. The order also prohibits the “submission of fictitious, fabricated, or hallucinated legal authority, statutes, quotations, or facts;” reliance on GenAI-generated citations “without independent verification;” “misrepresentation of GenAI-generated content as independently researched authority;” and “failure to disclose” GenAI use. Failure to comply with the order may result in sanctions including striking the filing, denial of relief, monetary sanctions, contempt proceedings, and referral to the Florida Bar.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Peters v. Banner Health, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 10367 (S.D. Tex. Jan. 15, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly submitted materials with false quotations and hallucinated case citations to the court, even after receiving “multiple admonishments.” The court concluded these erroneous filings “appear to be the product of [plaintiff’s] use of [GenAI]” use and noted that the plaintiff admitted to using ChatGPT in a prior filing. As a result of these hallucinations, the court did not prohibit GenAI use but reminded the plaintiff of her Rule 11 obligations and ordered that no document generated with GenAI may be filed unless she ensures that all citations are real and support the propositions asserted. The court also warned that violations could trigger sanctions up to and including dismissal. In so doing, the court noted the perils of submitting fake citations to the court, “including wasting the opposing party's time and money, the Court's time and resources, and reputational harms to the legal system.” After the plaintiff ignored these warnings and orders from the court, and continued to submit false quotes and citations, the court then required her to verify citations using the courthouse law library and attach a certification to all filings indicating she had done so. However, when the plaintiff also failed to include that required certification in various submissions, the court ultimately recommended dismissing her claims with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: Judge Blumenfeld has updated his standing orders for civil cases to require parties using GenAI to “generate any portion of” a court filing, to submit a separately filed document disclosing the use of GenAI and certifying the review and accuracy of any AI-generated content. This language appears to be specific to the use of GenAI in drafting court filings, and does not appear to apply to the use of GenAI for research or other supportive, non-drafting tasks.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Boyd v. Lee, 2026 Md. App. LEXIS 41 (Md. App. Jan. 14, 2026), the appellant’s counsel cited to “‘hallucinated’ and unsubstantiated case law” in his brief. The court identified that “[o]f the 14 cases cited in [his] brief, at least [six] contain a citation irregularity.” Counsel was ordered to show cause why he should not be sanctioned and referred to the Attorney Grievance Commission for violating the Maryland Attorney’s Rules of Professional Conduct. Although the attorney responded to the order to show cause, he neglected to respond to the court’s other prior orders to show cause (both requiring him to file paper copies of the brief). The court dismissed the appeal on other grounds and did not state whether it would ultimately refer the attorney to the Grievance Commission.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Douglas R. M. Nazarian | Judge Terrence Mark Ranko Zic | Senior Judge James A. Kenney III

rg_summary: In Hang Zhang v. Driscoll, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 8654 (N.D. Cal. Jan. 14, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “multiple citations to fictitious cases” in her reply brief in support of her motion for preliminary injunction. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff maintained that the citations were of real cases. When the court ordered the plaintiff to file copies of the cases, the plaintiff claimed the citations instead resulted from editing errors and attached two published cases. Judge Martínez-Olguín compared the cases and concluded that the “form and content of both are too far apart to plausibly result from a mere drafting error.” The court further concluded that citing to fictitious cases and the plaintiff’s subsequent dishonesty violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court sanctioned the plaintiff in the amount of $500 and warned that further violations would result in “case-terminating sanctions.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Young v. City of Memphis, 2:2025cv02799 (W.D. Tenn. Jan. 14, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “unknowingly and unintentionally” recorded a status conference using Read AI, a Microsoft Teams feature. The defendants raised concerns that the plaintiff’s conduct was intentional and had occurred previously. Judge Lipman noted that the recording of a status conference violated Local Rule 83.2(a)(1), which prohibits the “recording of transmission of Court Proceedings.” The court ordered that the plaintiff’s counsel file an affidavit detailing whether they have recorded any other judicial or non-judicial proceeding using AI without first informing opposing counsel.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage

rg_summary: In Mavy v. Comm’r of Soc. Sec. Admin., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 157358 (D. Ariz. Aug. 14, 2025), Magistrate Judge Alison S. Bachus found that an opening brief that was “replete with citation-related deficiencies,” including non existent and otherwise deficient authorities, suggested possible use of GenAI. Magistrate Judge Bachus ordered counsel to show cause under Rule 11. Counsel’s response described a two layer internal review process, but acknowledged widespread citation problems. Magistrate Judge Bachus concluded that “well over the majority” of the brief’s citations were fabricated, misleading, or unsupported and that counsel failed to conduct a reasonable inquiry, thus warranting sanctions, including revoking the attorney’s pro hac vice status, striking the plaintiff’s opening brief, and ordering the attorney to notify her client and various courts about her errors—while expressly declining to find subjective bad faith. On review, Judge Lanham held (see 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 5981 (D. Ariz. Jan. 13, 2026)) that, although counsel acted recklessly in failing to ensure citation accuracy, sua sponte Rule 11 sanctions require conduct “akin to a contempt of court” (typically necessitating bad faith or the equivalent), which was not met here. Judge Lanham emphasized that warnings about GenAI use had been issued, that a two-layer review occurred, and that after problems surfaced counsel investigated other filings and took corrective steps—facts consistent with recklessness rather than bad faith—so she vacated the Rule 11 sanction in part. However, to protect the orderly administration of justice, the court left in place the striking of the opening brief and revocation of the filing attorney’s pro hac vice status, which she concluded were justified on non-Rule 11 grounds given the impaired administration of justice caused by a filing “riddled” with citation errors.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Doe v. United States, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 5356 (D.N.J. Jan. 12, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s filings contained content that “appear[ed]” to include GenAI-generated material with hallucinated or nonexistent legal authorities. The court therefore cautioned that any additional submissions must comply with Rule 11’s verification obligations, and reminded the parties that pro se parties remain subject to sanctions for failing to ensure that claims and legal contentions are warranted by existing law. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed the complaint without prejudice, denied emergency injunctive relief, and allowed thirty days for the plaintiff to amend the filing.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hardy v. Whitaker, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44834 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 12, 2026), the court recommended dismissing the pro se plaintiff’s civil rights action with prejudice under Fed. R. Civ. P. 41(b) and enjoining him from filing new actions without leave of court. The plaintiff, who had been filing lawsuits in Michigan’s federal district courts for over 20 years, flooded the docket with frivolous motions and unauthorized documents throughout the litigation, repeatedly violating court rules and prior orders. The court noted that the plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment included fake case citations, which the court suspected resulted from the plaintiff's use of GenAI. Magistrate Judge Stafford observed that the use of GenAI when drafting legal filings “is a very risky practice,” as litigants remain subject to Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2), and that “even in the absence of bad faith, Rule 11 sanctions for use of AI generated phantom cases may be warranted.” The court further noted that pro se plaintiffs are “increasingly likely to clog dockets with frivolous and improper documents.” Applying the factors for dismissal under Rule 41(b), the court found that the plaintiff had engaged in bad faith conduct by stubbornly resisting court orders, prejudicing defendants, and ignoring repeated warnings, noting that lesser sanctions would be ineffective given his in forma pauperis status. The court recommended that the plaintiff be enjoined from filing any new federal actions in the Eastern District of Michigan or against any state or federal judge, officer, or employee without first obtaining leave of court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Hayes v. Chipotle Mexican Grill, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 3792 (M.D. Fla. Jan. 9, 2026), the court found that the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss cited nonexistent authorities, which the court observed likely resulted from the use of GenAI. Judge Barger cautioned that misuse of AI may warrant sanctions under both the court’s inherent authority and Rule 11 (which applies equally to unrepresented litigants). The court warned that continued misrepresentation of the record or reliance on nonexistent authority will result in sanctions, emphasizing that even a pro se litigant must conform to both the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and the court’s local rules. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed the complaint without prejudice and granted leave to amend.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Deutsche Bank Natl. Trust Co. v. LeTennier, 2026 N.Y. App. Div. LEXIS 46, (3d Dep’t Jan. 8, 2026), the court confronted an appeal in which the defendant’s appellate court filings contained numerous fabricated legal authorities, which counsel “reluctantly” admitted was the result of the use of GenAI. Although acknowledging that GenAI can benefit the profession, the court stressed that GenAI use does not diminish an attorney’s obligations of candor and accuracy. Applying New York’s law on frivolous conduct (22 NYCRR § 130-1.1), the court found the submission of fabricated authorities to be “completely without merit” and therefore sanctionable, especially given that the misconduct continued “after [the attorney] was on notice of such issue.” Noting that it was the first New York appellate decision imposing sanctions for misuse of GenAI, the court levied $5,000 against defense counsel to deter future violations, and imposed an additional $2,500 penalty on counsel and $2,500 on defendant due to the frivolous nature of the appeal. The court affirmed the challenged orders while noting a history of defendant’s vexatious litigation tactics.

rg_summary: On January 8, 2026, Judge Ho issued an update to his Individual Practices in Civil Pro Se Cases, which apply to all civil cases involving pro se litigants. The new rule on the “Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence” requires filers to disclose GenAI use in court filings and certify that they have reviewed and verified any GenAI-created content.

rg_summary: In Hollinger v. Enlisted Ass’n of the Nat’l Guard of the United States, No. 1:25-cv-01349 (ECF 112) (E.D. Va. Jan. 7, 2026), the pro se plaintiff admitted using GenAI to generate legal authorities that do not exist, ultimately requiring the court to address fabricated and inaccurate citations that wasted judicial and party resources. Despite a prior order to show cause and a sworn declaration from the plaintiff promising to use only verifiable citations going forward, the plaintiff continued to file materials containing inaccurate quotations and page cites, and again relied on authorities that did not support the asserted propositions. The court also noted additional filings in a companion matter where the plaintiff also persisted with fabricated citations and doubled down in subsequent briefing, even after being put on notice about his GenAI misuse. Based on this ongoing misrepresentation of legal authorities and broader noncompliance, the court dismissed the action with prejudice, but declined to award fees and costs because no proper itemized fee application accompanied the defendant’s motion for fees and costs.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In his First Amended Standing Order & Chambers Practices (D. Nev. Jan. 5, 2026), Magistrate Judge Couvillier includes a section on Artificial Intelligence. The order provides that “[a]ny counsel or pro se party may use AI to draft documents or process discovery consistent with Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (Fed. R. Civ. P.), this Standing Order, and any other applicable legal or ethical obligations.” All filings must contain a certification indicating “whether or not AI was used to prepare or assist in preparing such filing.” Compliance with the order also requires parties to verify accuracy of all cases and assertions. Parties may not upload documents “filed under seal” or that “contain personal-data identifiers” onto any “open AI system.” The order also encourages parties to include protocols addressing AI in protective orders. Violation of the order may result in sanctions.
rg_tags: Any AI Usage, Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Magistrate Judge Maximiliano D. Couvillier III

rg_summary: In Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 852 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 5, 2026) the court denied both the pro se plaintiffs’ motions for reconsideration of sanctions. The court had previously sanctioned each plaintiff $300 for submitting to the court filings containing misrepresented cases after being warned about GenAI use (See Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234830 (E.D. Mich Dec. 2, 2025)). One plaintiff asked for reconsideration because he “uses Chat GPT [sic] as ‘assistive’ technology and cannot afford his $300 sanction,” and requested a reduced sanction or payment plan. The other asked for reconsideration because she purportedly “did not draft the filings at issue, did not use ChatGPT, did not commit any violation, [and] has extremely limited income.” To the former, Judge Behm noted that the court considered the plaintiff’s financial circumstances when imposing sanctions, and the plaintiff did not previously indicate that he would not be able to pay a fine. To the latter, Judge Behm reiterated that the fact that the plaintiff did not draft the specific filings does not absolve her of her duty under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. Furthermore, the court expressed concern that reducing or eliminating the monetary sanction for the plaintiff will cause her to “misunderstand the seriousness of her conduct.” Judge Behm noted that the plaintiffs immediately followed the Magistrate Judge’s warning “with the very conduct they were warned about,” increasing the court’s doubt that a nominal penalty would deter the wrongful conduct.

rg_summary: In Hylton v. Hylton, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 11758 (S.D. Fla. Jan. 5, 2026), several of the pro se plaintiff’s filings contained inaccurate citations and purported case quotations. The court reminded the plaintiff that Rule 11 creates an obligation to ensure that filings accurately represent existing law, and that relying on GenAI to create cases to support a proposition without verifying their accuracy may lead to sanctions.  On unrelated grounds, the court granted a defendant’s motion to dismiss, while denying the other defendants’ motions as moot.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Allen v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 1645 (D. Idaho Jan. 4, 2026), the court found that the pro se plaintiff’s motion to dismiss counterclaims, strike affirmative defenses, and stay or bifurcate contained “fabricated quotations [and] misrepresented authority.” The court expressly cautioned that “mistaken reliance on [GenAI] for legal research[] is not a defense to Rule 11 sanctions” and warned that future fabrications or mischaracterizations may trigger a show-cause order under Rule 11. On unrelated grounds, the court denied the motion to dismiss, denied the motion to strike defenses, and declined to stay or bifurcate.

rg_summary: In Sevilla v. Ross, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268240 (D.S.C. Dec. 31, 2025), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s filings have “many hallmarks of motions created using artificial intelligence or found on the internet” and warned the plaintiffs that they could be subject to sanctions for further “frivolous or inappropriate” filings. The court denied the plaintiff’s numerous pending motions without prejudice as moot.

rg_summary: In Silverberg v. Bonomo, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268189 (D. Nev. Dec. 30, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s briefings cited cases for propositions that had nothing to do with the actual opinions and included a nonexistent quotation. The court warned that “misrepresented citations, even if not intentional, will not be tolerated by the Court” and cautioned that future errors, if the result of misuse of GenAI, will result in sanctions. The court granted the defendants’ motion to quash service on other grounds.

rg_summary: In M.T. Real Est. Inv. Inc. v. Servis One, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268188 (D. Nev. Dec. 30, 2025), the plaintiff’s responses to the defendant’s motion to dismiss and motion to strike the first amended complaint contained “five cited authorities which [the court] could not locate.” In response to Judge Navarro’s order to show cause, plaintiff’s counsel admitted that the cases “were not in fact real” and contended that the errors were the result of his paralegal’s GenAI use. Although Judge Navarro found that counsel’s conduct did not violate Local Rule IA 11-8(d) (which only prohibits false statements made “knowingly,” and counsel maintained that “he did not know that the hallucinated authorities were fake), the court found that the plaintiff’s counsel did violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2) for failing to verify the accuracy of citations submitted to the court. After considering  “sanctions imposed by other courts for similar conduct, the need to deter the future use of fake authorities, and [plaintiff’s counsel’s] conduct in this case,” the court struck the plaintiff’s responses (which it noted had the result of making the defendants’ motion to strike “unopposed, which constitutes [plaintiff’s] consent to the granting of the Motion”); awarded the defendants attorney’s fees and costs for work related to the false citations; ordered the plaintiff’s counsel to serve a copy of the order on the plaintiff; and directed the clerk to serve a copy of the order on the Nevada State Bar Association. The court noted that it would have also granted the defendant’s motion to strike on the merits, and then granted one of the defendants’ motions to dismiss the complaint with prejudice.
